setting: Bistritz and the journey through Austria-Hungary into Transylvania, 1890s, railway travel and hotel lodging, evening and night, reflective and foreboding

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: Dracula.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: Chapter One.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: Jonathan Harker's Journal. Kept in shorthand.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: 3rd May. Bistritz. Left Munich at 8:35 in the evening, on the 1st of May, arriving at Vienna early next morning; should have arrived at 6:46, but the train was an hour late.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Buda-Pesth seems a wonderful place, from the glimpse which I got of it from the train and the little I could walk through the streets.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I feared to go very far from the station, as we had arrived late and would start as near the correct time as possible.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: Harker reflected on the transition from the familiar West into stranger, darker territory.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: The impression I had was that we were leaving the West and entering the East; the most western of splendid bridges over the Danube, which is here of noble width and depth, took us among the traditions of Turkish rule.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: We left in pretty good time, and came after nightfall to Klausenburgh. Here I stopped for the night at the Hotel Royale.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I had for dinner, or rather supper, a chicken done up some way with red pepper, which was very good but thirsty.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: He made a private note to remember the dish for Mina.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Mem., get recipe for Mina.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I asked the waiter what it was called.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: The waiter told him:

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: It was called paprika hendl, and as it was a national dish, he should be able to get it anywhere along the Carpathians.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I found my smattering of German very useful here; indeed, I don't know how I should be able to get on without it.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Having had some time at my disposal when in London, I had visited the British Museum, and made search among the books and maps in the library regarding Transylvania; it had struck me that some foreknowledge of the country could hardly fail to have some importance in dealing with a nobleman of that country.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: His research had prepared him — or so he believed.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I find that the district he named is in the extreme east of the country, just on the borders of three states, Transylvania, Moldavia and Bukovina, in the midst of the Carpathian mountains; one of the wildest and least known portions of Europe.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I was not able to light on any map or work giving the exact locality of the Castle Dracula, as there are no maps of this country as yet to compare with our own Ordnance Survey maps; but I found that Bistritz, the post town named by Count Dracula, is a fairly well-known place.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I shall enter here some of my notes, as they may refresh my memory when I talk over my travels with Mina.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: In the population of Transylvania there are four distinct nationalities: Saxons in the South, and mixed with them the Wallachs, who are the descendants of the Dacians; Magyars in the West, and Szekelys in the East and North.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I am going among the latter, who claim to be descended from Attila and the Huns. This may be so, for when the Magyars conquered the country in the eleventh century they found the Huns settled in it.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: Harker reflected on the history and legends of the people he was soon to meet.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I read that every known superstition in the world is gathered into the horseshoe of the Carpathians, as if it were the centre of some sort of imaginative whirlpool; if so my stay may be very interesting.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Mem., I must ask the Count all about them.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I did not sleep well, though my bed was comfortable enough, for I had all sorts of queer dreams.
blocks.setting_change: Hotel room in Bistritz, late night, restless and uneasy

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: There was a dog howling all night under my window, which may have had something to do with it; or it may have been the paprika, for I had to drink up all the water in my carafe, and was still thirsty.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Towards morning I slept and was wakened by the continuous knocking at my door, so I guess I must have been sleeping soundly then.

blocks.speaker: dracula_narrator
blocks.line: The strange night had left him unsettled.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Hm.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I had for breakfast more paprika, and a sort of porridge of maize flour which they said was mamaliga, and egg-plant stuffed with forcemeat, a very excellent dish, which they call impletata.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: Mem., get recipe for this also.

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: I had to hurry breakfast, for the train started a little before eight, or rather it ought to have done so, for after rushing to the station at 7:30 I had to sit in the carriage for more than an hour before we began to move.
blocks.setting_change: Railway station and train carriage, morning, busy and delayed

blocks.speaker: jonathan harker
blocks.line: It seems to me that the further east you go the more unpunctual are the trains. What ought they to be in China?
